glossary.definition: intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence. It has the following idea. There are 3 rooms. The tester does not see nor artificial intelligence, nor human being and asks questions to both intelligences using a text-based input tool. If the tester cannot differentiate which answers were given by the human being and which ones by the artificial intelligence, than the artificial intelligence is intelligent.

glossary.definition: a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. glossary.term: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information
glossary.definition: two players participate in the game and compete against each other. In the context of our course, these could be a human and an intelligent computer system, or two intelligent computer systems playing against each other; the players make alternate moves until the end of the game (i. e. the first player moves, the second player moves, the first player moves, and so on ); both players have access to complete information about the course of the game (including past moves, future moves, and the available objects (e. g. cards or figures) used in the game). Hence, no information is hidden from either player; randomness factors (e. g., the use of a die) are not involved in these types of games; one player’s loss is equal to another player’s gain, giving a total sum of zero.
glossary.sentence: In this course, to demonstrate the application of search-based problem solving to the representation and implementation of game playing, we will focus on a relatively simple type of game: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information . The following aspects characterise this type of game: two players participate in the game and compete against each other. In the context of our course, these could be a human and an intelligent computer system, or two intelligent computer systems playing against each other; the players make alternate moves until the end of the game (i. e. the first player moves, the second player moves, the first player moves, and so on ); both players have access to complete information about the course of the game (including past moves, future moves, and the available objects (e. g. cards or figures) used in the game). Hence, no information is hidden from either player; randomness factors (e. g., the use of a die) are not involved in these types of games; one player’s loss is equal to another player’s gain, giving a total sum of zero.

glossary.definition: It means that it is not enough to provide an intelligent computer system with a state space graph. It also needs a search algorithm. Therefore, an intelligent computer system needs a state space graph to be able to solve problems. It is a data structure organizing and storing data about states and actions of the problem-solving process. Additionally, an intelligent computer system needs a search algorithm that processes the data structure to find a solution to the problem based on known input data (Figure 1).
glossary.sentence: Topic 2.3 - State Space Search The last topics have explained how humans solve problems and how a problem-solving process is represented in an intelligent computer system by using a state space graph. However, when solving a problem, an individual, based on his/her own subjective criteria, chooses the most appropriate problem-solving alternative from all possible alternatives and performs actions included in this alternative to find a solution to the problem. Therefore, a logical question would be – what else does an intelligent computer system need to be able to find a solution to the problem if it already has a formal description of the problem-solving process? It means that it is not enough to provide an intelligent computer system with a state space graph. It also needs a search algorithm. Therefore, an intelligent computer system needs a state space graph to be able to solve problems. It is a data structure organizing and storing data about states and actions of the problem-solving process. Additionally, an intelligent computer system needs a search algorithm that processes the data structure to find a solution to the problem based on known input data (Figure 1).

glossary.definition: problem-specific knowledge that very often is based on our experience.
glossary.sentence: In the real world, we, as humans, have a lot of problems to solve in our everyday or professional lives, such as playing games, managing our finances, and addressing medical issues. To solve problems, we use problem-specific knowledge called heuristic knowledge that very often is based on our experience.

glossary.definition: Both strategies described above use a heuristic evaluation function. In further text, let’s consider guidelines for developing a heuristic evaluation function for a game.
glossary.sentence: Both strategies described above use a heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: numerical values called heuristic measures . Heuristic measures are assigned to nodes of the state space graph. This means that the heuristic knowledge that we usually formulate verbally should be represented mathematically and made accessible by the intelligent computer system through heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge is incorporated in the heuristic evaluation function, which calculates numerical values called heuristic measures . Heuristic measures are assigned to nodes of the state space graph. This means that the heuristic knowledge that we usually formulate verbally should be represented mathematically and made accessible by the intelligent computer system through heuristic measures.

glossary.definition: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: However, in practical applications, the previously mentioned elements of the heuristic evaluation function are represented indirectly by focusing on factors characterizing states in the problem-solving process and their importance for reaching the goal state (Figure 3). As a result, the heuristic evaluation function can be represented as a polynomial function, as used by Samuel in his checker program ((Samuel, 1959) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): where n – a node under consideration; ai – the weight of the ith factor; xi – the heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: the weight of the ith factor
glossary.sentence: As a result, the heuristic evaluation function can be represented as a polynomial function, as used by Samuel in his checker program ((Samuel, 1959) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): where n – a node under consideration; ai – the weight of the ith factor; xi – the heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process.

glossary.sentence: As a result, the heuristic evaluation function can be represented as a polynomial function, as used by Samuel in his checker program ((Samuel, 1959) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): where n – a node under consideration; ai – the weight of the ith factor; xi – the heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: the average number of arcs per node in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: General, the complexity of a state space graph depends on two factors (Luger, 2009): B – the branching factor of the state space graph . It is the average number of arcs per node. Usually, we acquire this factor by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes with the total number of nodes in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: the length of the longest path from the root node to a leaf node in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: General, the complexity of a state space graph depends on two factors (Luger, 2009): B – the branching factor of the state space graph . It is the average number of arcs per node. Usually, we acquire this factor by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes with the total number of nodes in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: When a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, and this may lead to a bad situation later in the game. As a result, this may cause the entire game to be lost!
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: a state space graph that has been truncated due to limited computational resources, so only a portion of the graph is available for exploration.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, depending on the type of problem, the search algorithm may have one of the following available: Only a part of a state space graph (a partly available state space graph) . It is not possible to store and process the whole graph due to limited computational resources. As a result, during the operation of an intelligent computer system, the state space graph is generated to the specified depth that is allowed by available computational resources.

glossary.definition: a state space graph where all nodes and arcs are stored and processed.
glossary.sentence: A full state space graph (a fully available state space graph) . Computational resources are enough to store and process such a graph. For example, in planning problems that must be solved before a robot starts acting in some environment, an action plan is drawn up and represented as a state space graph. In this case, the use of the heuristic evaluation function is not always necessary.

glossary.definition: a characteristic of some states in a problem that are essentially the same despite having different labels or representations.
glossary.sentence: One of the suggestions is to use a symmetry feature of states, which can be done for the game of Tic-tac-toe (Figure 12).

glossary.definition: situations on a board where two adjacent tiles need to be swapped.
glossary.sentence: This means that we need to change the places of two tiles located close one to another, and usually, it takes more than two moves to put them back. In our case, all three nodes will receive a value of 0, because there are no such situations on the board.

glossary.definition: a heuristic evaluation function that calculates the total distance between each tile and its goal position.
glossary.sentence: We can use each of them alone as a heuristic evaluation function, for example, f(n) = the number of tiles out of place f(n) = the sum of distances f(n) = the number of tile reversals. We can make combinations of these functions to create more complex heuristic evaluation functions, such as f(n) = the number of tiles out of place + the number of tile reversals f(n) = the number of tiles out of place + the sum of distances + the number of tile reversals.

glossary.term: number of tiles out of place
glossary.definition: a heuristic evaluation function that counts the number of tiles that are not in their correct positions on a board.
glossary.sentence: We can use each of them alone as a heuristic evaluation function, for example, f(n) = the number of tiles out of place f(n) = the sum of distances f(n) = the number of tile reversals. We can make combinations of these functions to create more complex heuristic evaluation functions, such as f(n) = the number of tiles out of place + the number of tile reversals f(n) = the number of tiles out of place + the sum of distances + the number of tile reversals.

glossary.definition: small adjustments of weights after each training example to reduce the difference between the actual and desired output
glossary.sentence: The learning is based on the small adjustments of weights after each training example to reduce the difference between the actual and desired output ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a data structure used to represent all possible moves in a game.
glossary.sentence: At the first level of the game tree, it is possible to consider only three states: corner, centre, and side, because, for example, it does not matter in which corner the sign is placed.

glossary.definition: a heuristic search algorithm that always moves to the node with the best heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: an algorithm that always chooses a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node if the best heuristic measure is defined as the highest measure, and vice versa—if the best heuristic measure is defined as the smallest measure, the algorithm will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is less than the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: Thus, if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the highest measure, then Hill Climbing will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node, and vice versa—if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the smallest measure, the algorithm will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is less than the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction).
glossary.sentence: one of the shortcomings of Hill Climbing is the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: another shortcoming of Hill Climbing is the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: an algorithm that does not have the shortcomings of Hill Climbing; it selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before. This allows the algorithm to switch between different paths in the state space graph, and it does not lose a problem solution if the heuristic evaluation function is not well-defined.
glossary.sentence: The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings. Let’s consider it. In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before.

glossary.definition: a data structure that works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7). The priority queue works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.

glossary.definition: an algorithm that provides a kind of compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search; it selects a limited number of best nodes for further expansion in each iteration. The number of the best nodes examined during search is set before the algorithm starts searching. This number is called a beam width.
glossary.sentence: Let’s consider an algorithm providing a kind of compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search. Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before.

glossary.definition: the number of nodes that are considered at each iteration.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm stops when the search goal node is added to the OPEN list or when leaf nodes with heuristic measures better than the heuristic measure of the search goal node are reached. The algorithm does not backtrack in case of failure. Let us consider an example of Beam Search by implementing data-driven search on the state space graph that you can see in Figure 9. The search start node is A. The search goal node is K. The beam width is 2 nodes.

glossary.definition: a value that estimates the quality of a node.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic measures are given close to nodes, and a smaller heuristic measure represents a better quality of node.

glossary.definition: A representation of all possible game states and transitions between them.
glossary.sentence: In the case of a complete state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the root node is called a game result .

glossary.definition: A representation of the game states where not all possible future states are explicitly shown.
glossary.sentence: In the case of the partial state space graph, heuristic measures of nodes are obtained by using a heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: the player whose task is to maximise his or her result in the game.
glossary.sentence: The task of the maximiser is to maximise his or her result in the game.

glossary.definition: the player whose task is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, that is, to prevent the maximiser from winning the game.
glossary.sentence: The task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, i.e. to prevent the maximiser from winning the game.

glossary.definition: The outcome of the game if both players play optimally.
glossary.sentence: In the case of a complete state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the root node is called a game result .

glossary.definition: A sequence of nodes in the state space graph that leads to a win for a player.
glossary.sentence: In the case of the complete state space graph, winning paths are identified based on the game result. These are not paths that definitely lead to a win by a particular player; they are paths showing the course of the game that is most likely to be undertaken.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm used in game theory to find the optimal move for a player, assuming that the opponent will also play optimally.
glossary.sentence: We generally apply the minimax and alpha-beta pruning algorithms to process the complete state space graph of the game, and N-ply look-ahead and Iterative deepening to the partial state space graph. Let’s consider these algorithms.

glossary.definition: An optimization technique for the minimax algorithm that reduces the number of nodes evaluated, improving search efficiency.
glossary.sentence: Alpha-beta pruning improves the search efficiency by reducing the number of nodes evaluated and consequently the time spent searching for a solution. Let’s consider this approach.

glossary.definition: A value used in alpha-beta pruning to track the best move found so far at a given node level (MAX level).
glossary.sentence: Two numbers are used in alpha-beta pruning (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009): An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels. This value should not decrease, since this would represent a bad move.

glossary.definition: A value used in alpha-beta pruning to track the worst move found so far at a given node level (MIN level).
glossary.sentence: Two numbers are used in alpha-beta pruning (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009): An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels. This value should not decrease, since this would represent a bad move.

glossary.definition: Performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the β value) is −1, and the measure of the ancestor (the α value) is 1. By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: a measure assigned when it is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure. It indicates a range of possible values for the heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm assigns a precise heuristic measure that is equal to the heuristic measure of the leaf node, since the node at this level do not have other descendants.

glossary.definition: Based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far.
glossary.sentence: Step 15: After making the cut off, the node whose unconsidered paths were cut off acquires a precise heuristic measure based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The measure of the ancestor.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: Performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the two nodes have only one common value of 1 or, in another words, the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: The current node is located at the MIN level
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MIN level, but there is no difference between the heuristic measures of the descendants.

glossary.definition: The node is located at the MAX level
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher, since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm goes down from the current state of the game to the terminal nodes located at the predefined depth. It applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes. Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner – at each iteration, it goes one level deeper (Rich et al., 2009)
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening can be used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game.

glossary.definition: “A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some task T and some performance measure P, if its performance on T, as measured by P, improves with experience E.” The description provided by SAS (n. d.) explains the concept in straightforward words: "Machine learning is a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. It is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.” Negnevitsky (2011) points to several possible ways of learning:
glossary.sentence: Nowadays, machine learning is a highly topical area in the field of computer science. Therefore, there are plenty of definitions of this concept.

glossary.definition: the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information (Tucker & Belford, 2019).
glossary.sentence: Computer science is the broadest concept in this context.

glossary.definition: a type of explicit learning. Supervised learning is similar to learning letters or numbers in school: the teacher shows and pronounces a letter or a number, he/she repeats this process several times, and, in the end, children can recognise different letters and numbers. According to (Atul, 2019), this type of learning can be characterised as "Train me!". In supervised learning, input data (the training set) already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019). For example, imagine a dataset representing animals by a set of features (like the existence of tail, height, body shape, etc.). In this case, labels will indicate if a specific data object is a cat, a dog or any other animal. Therefore, supervised learning aims to find a rule (mapping) describing a relationship between input data and output (Geron, 2017). A mathematical model represents this rule. Thus, supervised learning is based on predictive models because it predicts the output for a new data object based on the model trained on the training set.
glossary.sentence: Supervised learning is also called learning with a teacher.

glossary.definition: a type of learning based on unlabelled data. It can be characterised as "I am self-sufficient in learning!". The goal of this learning is to reveal patterns/structure of data. Unsupervised learning provides descriptive models. It is also used for data visualisation.
glossary.sentence: This type of learning is based on unlabelled data (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019). We can compare it with a child that has a set of bricks of different colours in front of him. Looking at them, he/she starts dividing them into different groups based on their colours.

glossary.definition: explicitly programmed by their developers. It means that the developers code each step of an algorithm in a precise way together with possible inputs and outputs.
glossary.sentence: Conventional algorithms are explicitly programmed by their developers.

glossary.definition: an individual entity or observation in a dataset
glossary.sentence: For example, we have a dataset characterising countries by the number of state-financed athletes in each country (Table 2). In this case, the number of state-financed athletes is a feature ( x ) describing a data object “country”.

glossary.definition: a collection of data points, each representing an individual object or observation
glossary.sentence: There is a dataset with data collected in the past. The dataset contains the known values of one input variable x , representing a specific feature, i.e. a data object is characterised by one feature (Table 1).

glossary.definition: a specific characteristic or attribute of a data object
glossary.sentence: For example, input variables can represent values of different house properties like the house area, the number of floors, land area under the house, etc. Linear regression in this case would provide a prediction of the house price.

glossary.definition: a specific attribute or characteristic of a data object.
a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: A feature set – a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.

glossary.definition: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A model - a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: a result of applying a model to given data objects.
glossary.sentence: A model output - a result of applying a model to given data objects.

glossary.definition: an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.
glossary.sentence: A parameter (also called a model parameter) - an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.
glossary.sentence: A test set – a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.

glossary.definition: the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Testing – the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.
glossary.sentence: Training - the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.
glossary.sentence: A training set - a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.

glossary.definition: deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints; noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal; non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables and others.

glossary.definition: is carried out when labels of data objects are presented as real values (so they are continuous) as the price of a house, the number of graduating students, etc. Therefore, for a new data object, the machine learning algorithm finds a prediction, for example, the prediction of the possible price of a house, the prediction of the potential number of graduating students, etc.
glossary.sentence: Regression is carried out when labels of data objects are presented as real values (so they are continuous) as the price of a house, the number of graduating students, etc.

glossary.definition: is reward-based learning. According to (Atul, 2019), it can be characterised as "My life-my rules!". Reinforcement learning is suitable for agents (like robots, self-driving cars, or a game-playing software) who operate in a changing environment and, to act in the environment, they need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal (Pant, 2019). Therefore, the agent learns how to act in the environment. The agent receives feedback: a reward if it acts correctly and a penalty if it acts incorrectly (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019). The reward is represented as a numerical value but it is given with some delay, like at the end of the game when it is known who has won the game: computer or human. The agent learns without intervention from human, for example, the two points for a win at the end of a chess game tells the agent it did something right and, thus, it is up to the agent to decide which of the actions before the reinforcement were most responsible for it (Russell & Norvig, 2010). For example, DeepMind's AlphaGo program learned its optimal gaming policy by analysing millions of games and then playing many games against itself (Geron, 2017).
glossary.sentence: Summarising the content of this topic, it is worth emphasising again that machine learning is about developing a mathematical model of data. Nowadays, there are many tools, frameworks, and libraries available for building machine learning models automatically. Using the existing tools and libraries, the developer of a machine learning model loads data and defines hyperparameters but rarely needs to write a machine learning algorithm from scratch. glossary.definition: a process of dividing an available dataset into subsets (clusters) that share some similarities.

finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019), for example, running an association rule analysis on your sales logs may reveal that people who purchase barbecue sauce and potato chips also tend to buy steak (Geron, 2017).
glossary.sentence: The typical tasks belonging to unsupervised learning are the following: Clustering deals with segmenting data into representative classes called clusters (Jones, 2008; Pant, 2019). Therefore, data objects belonging to a specific cluster share some common characteristics, while characteristics differ between clusters. Usually, the grouping of data objects is based on the distance between points or their similarity metrics. However, clustering algorithms cannot say what each cluster is about; they can only separate similar data points from others (Atul, 2019).  Association analysis finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019), for example, running an association rule analysis on your sales logs may reveal that people who purchase barbecue sauce and potato chips also tend to buy steak (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019), for example, a car's mileage may be significantly correlated with its age, so the dimensionality reduction algorithm will merge them into one feature that represents the car's wear and tear (Geron, 2017).
glossary.sentence: The typical tasks belonging to unsupervised learning are the following: Clustering deals with segmenting data into representative classes called clusters (Jones, 2008; Pant, 2019). Therefore, data objects belonging to a specific cluster share some common characteristics, while characteristics differ between clusters. Usually, the grouping of data objects is based on the distance between points or their similarity metrics. However, clustering algorithms cannot say what each cluster is about; they can only separate similar data points from others (Atul, 2019).  Association analysis finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019), for example, running an association rule analysis on your sales logs may reveal that people who purchase barbecue sauce and potato chips also tend to buy steak (Geron, 2017). Dimensionality reduction is associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019), for example, a car's mileage may be significantly correlated with its age, so the dimensionality reduction algorithm will merge them into one feature that represents the car's wear and tear (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: a variable that is assumed to influence or cause changes in another variable.
glossary.sentence: For example, we can assume that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between the education level of an employee (an independent variable) and his/her salary (a dependent variable), i.e., we assume that the salary depends on the education level: the higher is the education level, the higher is the salary.

glossary.definition: a variable whose value is believed to be influenced or determined by one or more independent variables.
glossary.sentence: For example, we can assume that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between the education level of an employee (an independent variable) and his/her salary (a dependent variable), i.e., we assume that the salary depends on the education level: the higher is the education level, the higher is the salary.

glossary.definition: having a linear pattern and thus can be represented by a straight line in the two-dimensional space
glossary.sentence: “Linear” means that the relationship expected between the two mentioned variables has a linear pattern and thus can be represented by a straight line in the two-dimensional space, as shown in Figure 1.

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression. It assumes the linear relationship between the input variables and the single output variable. Thus, it uses the linear function. From the given training dataset, it learns the values of weights used in the linear function.
glossary.sentence: In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset, input values are weight values of the linear function and independent variables are presented by a feature vector. The gradient descent technique thus allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function. It is a supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression. It assumes the linear relationship between the input variables (values of data object features) and the single output variable (a prediction). Thus, it uses the linear function. From the given training dataset, it learns the values of weights used in the linear function.

glossary.definition: feature values of data objects
glossary.sentence: Linear regression is a supervised machine learning algorithm that assumes a linear relationship between the input variables and the single output variable. Input variables are feature values of data objects. The output variable is a prediction made by the linear regression model.

glossary.definition: a prediction made by the linear regression model
glossary.sentence: Linear regression is a supervised machine learning algorithm that assumes a linear relationship between the input variables and the single output variable. Input variables are feature values of data objects. The output variable is a prediction made by the linear regression model.

glossary.definition: a rule or pattern that describes how two or more variables are related to each other
glossary.sentence: It means that we need to find a mathematical relationship between x and y hidden in the dataset that we already have, i.e., we need to understand the relationship between the number of state-financed athletes and the number of victories at world tournaments.

glossary.definition: a relationship between two variables that can be represented by a straight line
glossary.sentence: In mathematics, the linear relationship between two variables x and y is represented by the following function: y=m*x +b, where x – the value of the input variable, y – the value of the output variable, m – a real number that corresponds to the slope of the line, b – a real number that corresponds to the intercept (a point where the line crosses the y axis or, in other words, it is the value of y when x is equal to 0).

glossary.definition: the steepness of a line

glossary.definition: the point where a line crosses the y-axis

glossary.definition: the difference between the output value predicted by the linear regression model and the real output value
glossary.sentence: The difference between the output value predicted by the linear regression model and the real output value is called an error .

glossary.sentence: The linear regression model uses a loss function called squared loss (also known as L2 loss) (Google Developers, n.d.; Russell and Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: a type of linear regression that uses multiple input features to predict the output variable
glossary.sentence: There are two types of linear regression (Russell and Norvig, 2010): univariate (or simple) linear regression – when only one single feature is used to represent a data object and the simple equation of the linear regression model is used multivariate (multiple) linear regression – when each data object is represented by multiple features and the more complex equation of the linear regression model is used.

glossary.sentence: Therefore, usually, another mechanism is used, which is called gradient descent. The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values.

glossary.definition: A derivative of a function with respect to one variable, while holding all other variables constant.
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables.

glossary.definition: A measure of the average squared difference between the predicted output and the actual output.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.

glossary.definition: A type of classification task where there are two possible outcomes or classes.

glossary.definition: A type of classification task where there are more than two possible outcomes or classes.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives.

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm used for classification tasks, especially binary classification. It predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs to.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression , in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task. Therefore, it predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs to, for example, success/failure, good/bad, yes/no, win/loss, etc.

glossary.definition: A function that squashes the output of a linear function to a range between 0 and 1. It is used in logistic regression to determine the probability of a data object belonging to a particular class.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression also receives as input data the values of the features of data objects, but outputs a binary class label of this object as a result. For example, the input data may be data on the student's average grade in the semester work and the number of missed lectures. Logistic regression in this case would determine the chances of this student to pass or fail the study course. The dataset used in the logistic regression is similar to the data set shown in Table 3, with one exception: labels of data objects are represented as classes. For example, we have a dataset about students' average marks in the semester work and the number of missed lectures (Table 5). In addition, the dataset contains labels showing if a specific student passed or failed the study course in the past. It means that we have two classes in our dataset: pass (1) and fail (0). Based on these data, we want to acquire a mathematical model that will predict whether students of the current and subsequent years will pass or fail the study course. Logistic regression also tries finding a mathematical function that relates x to y . It also uses the linear function as the linear regression does. Still, after receiving the linear function's value, it applies a sigmoid function to squash the value of teh linear function within the range of [0,1] using the sigmoid function.

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks. It classifies data points based on the class labels of their k nearest neighbours.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks. Let’s define the idea of this algorithm based on the classification task.

glossary.definition: a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1. The learning rate determines how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning.
glossary.sentence: A learning rate is one of the hyperparameters used in artificial neural networks. It is a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1. The learning rate determines how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning.

glossary.definition: an algorithm that stores all available data and classifies new data points based on a similarity measure (e.g., distance).

The concept of similarity (sometimes called distance, proximity, or closeness) makes the basis for the algorithm. In this case, the similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity) (Kubat, 2017). The notion of similarity differs for different data types (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.sentence: For continuous feature values, it is possible to calculate the geometric distance between any pair of data objects: the closer to each other the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

used to calculate the distance between two points plotted in a grid

glossary.definition: In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset (Jones, 2009).
glossary.sentence: In general, we can differentiate two versions of the algorithm. In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.
glossary.sentence: In general, we can differentiate two versions of the algorithm. In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset (Jones, 2009). In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification) (Russell & Norvig, 2010).
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification) (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

parameters that are set before the training process and control the learning process of an algorithm.
a subset of data objects within a dataset that share similar characteristics.
the centre of a cluster, representing the average of the feature vectors within that cluster.
a popular unsupervised machine learning algorithm that partitions data into K clusters by iteratively assigning data points to the nearest centroid.
a bottom-up clustering approach that starts with each data object as a separate cluster and iteratively merges the closest clusters until all objects are in one cluster.
glossary.sentence: Figure 2 represents the process of agglomerative hierarchical clustering in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering.

glossary.definition: a top-down clustering approach that starts with all data objects in one cluster and recursively splits clusters into two new clusters until each data object is in its own cluster.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it.

glossary.definition: methods used to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: a linkage method that calculates the distance between two clusters as the longest distance between any pair of objects from the two clusters.
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: a linkage method that calculates the distance between two clusters as the shortest distance between any pair of objects from the two clusters.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: a linkage method that calculates the distance between two clusters as the average distance between all pairs of objects from the two clusters.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: a horizontal line drawn through a dendrogram to determine the number of clusters.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.definition: a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.
glossary.sentence: Predictions made in the classification task can lead to four types of outcomes (Kubat, 2017) : Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.

glossary.definition: a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.
glossary.sentence: Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam. True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.

glossary.definition: a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as positive.
glossary.sentence: Erroneous predictions: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as positive.

glossary.definition: a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as negative.
glossary.sentence: Erroneous predictions: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as positive. False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as negative.

glossary.definition: It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010) . Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category.

It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017) : Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: It measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve. It returns a value in the range [0, 1]. A highly accurate classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 1; in contrast, a purely random classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 0.5.
glossary.sentence: AUC can be calculated for each class vs other classes for the multiclass classification task and then averaged across all classes.

glossary.definition: Data objects that are actually positive but classified as negative.
glossary.sentence: Kubat (2017) gives an example of a medical diagnosis, in which a patient suffering from X illness but not diagnosed as such represents a false negative, something the doctor wants to avoid—which means that recall should be small.

glossary.definition: Data objects that are classified as positive but are actually negative.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives.

glossary.definition: A metric used in classification tasks that represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: The average of the precision values calculated for each class in a multiclass classification task.
glossary.sentence: After that, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.

glossary.definition: The average of the recall values calculated for each class in a multiclass classification task.
glossary.sentence: After that, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates the accuracy of positive predictions.
glossary.sentence: In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017) . Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017) .

glossary.definition: The inherent relationship between precision and recall where increasing one metric often leads to a decrease in the other.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff .

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates the fraction of actual positive data objects that are correctly identified.
glossary.sentence: According to (Kubat, 2017) , recall is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: A graphical representation of the performance of a classifier at various threshold settings.
glossary.sentence: The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff (Russell & Norvig, 2010; Geron, 2017; Kubat, 2017)

glossary.definition: A metric used to evaluate the quality of clustering by measuring how similar each data point is to its own cluster compared to other clusters.
glossary.sentence: One of the metrics that do not demand ground-truth labels is the Silhouette Score . The Silhouette Score is calculated by the following way: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster), b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters).

glossary.definition: Data objects that are actually positive and correctly classified as positive.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a recall for each specific class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row corresponding to the class as false negatives.

glossary.definition: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig. Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons. The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig. Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons. The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron.

glossary.definition: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron (Ertel, 2011; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011), which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks. In general, an artificial neuron has (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals) which are represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons. The numerical values from the environment typically correspond to the values of data object features in the dataset. The inputs typically are labelled by indexed x; a set of real-valued weights (one for each input) that express the strength, or, in other words, the importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output.

glossary.definition: accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons
glossary.sentence: It has ( Negnevitsky , 2011): an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons; an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons; at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features. Neurons in the hidden layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour, and there is no obvious way to know what the desired output of the hidden layer should be.

glossary.definition: connections only in one direction; every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection . Every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops.

glossary.definition: feeds its outputs back into its inputs; the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs. It means that the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour.

glossary.definition: related to modifying weights in some particular way; after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output. In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network
glossary.sentence: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way . It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output. glossary.definition: There are two groups: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: Intelligent computer systems that learn from examples.

glossary.definition: Intelligent computer systems that utilize search algorithms for planning.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning applications and search-based planning systems are examples of such systems.

glossary.definition: The process of obtaining knowledge for a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows methods and tools of artificial intelligence and how to describe and represent knowledge in a knowledge base, working with domain experts.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A person with deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain

glossary.definition: Knowledge that allows a domain expert to solve problems more effectively.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: An automated system used by domain experts or knowledge engineers to describe knowledge and build a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system that emulates a human expert's methodology and performance in a narrow specialized domain.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems

glossary.definition: Formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: Represent knowledge in the form of logical statements using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and inference rules.
glossary.sentence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: Represent knowledge as a directed labelled graph where nodes represent objects/concepts and arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them;

glossary.definition: Represent knowledge as a graph where nodes are complex structures containing all characteristics and components of an object/concept.
glossary.sentence: structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept;

glossary.term: Procedural or Rule-Based Knowledge Representation Schemas
glossary.definition: Represent knowledge as a set of procedures or instructions.
glossary.sentence: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph with nodes representing concepts and arcs representing relations between them.
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them

glossary.definition: A relation represented by a title of “is a”, “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”. It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts.
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to.
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to (Fig.7).

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title of “Part of”. It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object.
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of” (Fig.8). It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties.
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties (Fig.9).

glossary.definition: A characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values.
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values (Fig.10).

glossary.definition: They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: An example of structured knowledge representation schemas.
