This is a remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary.” (Minsky, 1975).
glossary.sentence: Frames are an example of structured knowledge representation schemas. Marvin Minsky developed frames in 1975. They are based on the following idea: “When one encounters a new situation (or makes a substantial change in one's view of the present problem) one selects from memory a structure called a Frame. This is a remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary.” (Minsky, 1975)

glossary.definition: a way to store knowledge about an object in a structured way; provides hierarchical organization of knowledge and allows representation of various knowledge about objects.
glossary.sentence: Frames have the following advantages: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects;

glossary.definition: a characteristic or attribute of an object stored in a frame.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine searches for the specified property of the object until its value is obtained ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.
glossary.sentence: Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.

glossary.definition: an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a template describing a general category of objects.
glossary.sentence: First, there are two high-level class frames: Person and Animal .

glossary.definition: a specific realization of a class frame.
glossary.sentence: First, there are two high-level class frames: Person and Animal . Next, there are two sub-class frames: Cat and Dog . They inherit slots from the Animal class frame, and each of them has new slots added: Affectionate in the Cat sub-class and Roar and Barking in the Dog sub-class. Finally, there are four instance frames: TreeAlex and BrownLucie are instances for the Person class frame; Tom is an instance for the Cat class frame; Rex is an instance for the Dog class frame.

glossary.definition: A unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame
glossary.sentence: A referencing principle is used to relate two frames. This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.
glossary.sentence: Generalization relation . It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.

glossary.definition: It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole .
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole .

glossary.definition: It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011). In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons.
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011). We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.
glossary.sentence: We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.

glossary.definition: the executable part of a frame; code describing what will happen when the demon is called.
glossary.sentence: Although the demons are triggered automatically, their executable part (the code describing what will happen when the demon is called) should be developed by the developer of the intelligent computer system.

glossary.definition: it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;

glossary.definition: it is triggered when a slot value changes
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes;

glossary.definition: it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame;

glossary.definition: it is triggered when a slot value is deleted
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame; IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted

glossary.definition: a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem; has an IF condition and a THEN consequence.
glossary.sentence: The simplest type of rules is a production rule . Each production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a rule-based expert system consisting of a knowledge base, working memory, and inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory;

glossary.definition: the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory
glossary.sentence: Thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory

glossary.definition: a description of the current state of the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”
glossary.sentence: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”

glossary.definition: the set of enabled rules
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set

glossary.definition: the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set

glossary.definition: In data-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its THEN part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when the search goal is found. Figure 3 represents the idea of forward chaining. Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001). However, the intelligent computer system may execute many rules in forward chaining that have nothing to do with the established goal ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: In data-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its THEN part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when the search goal is found. Figure 3 represents the idea of forward chaining. Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001). However, the intelligent computer system may execute many rules in forward chaining that have nothing to do with the established goal ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its IF part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when facts about the problem are found. Backward chaining corresponds to a decomposition of the problem’s goal into simpler subgoals; thus, backward search in an expert system corresponds roughly to the process of hypothesis testing in human problem solving (Luger, 2009). Figure 4 represents the idea of backward chaining. Goal-driven search is typically used in tasks of diagnosis when it is known what happened and it is necessary to understand reasons for it.
glossary.sentence: In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its IF part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when facts about the problem are found. Backward chaining corresponds to a decomposition of the problem’s goal into simpler subgoals; thus, backward search in an expert system corresponds roughly to the process of hypothesis testing in human problem solving (Luger, 2009). Figure 4 represents the idea of backward chaining. Goal-driven search is typically used in tasks of diagnosis when it is known what happened and it is necessary to understand reasons for it.

glossary.definition: each rule can be written so that it has an explicitly stated priority value
glossary.sentence: Rules can be written so that each has an explicitly stated priority value, and, thus, if more than one rule can fire, the one with the highest priority is chosen

glossary.definition: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one
glossary.sentence: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one

glossary.definition: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.
